title: Listening - Part 3: Starting a Small Business
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a challenge they faced when starting a small business. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main challenge each speaker describes. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a challenge they faced when starting a small business. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main challenge each speaker describes. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about setting up a small business.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I started making decorated cakes for birthdays after friends kept asking me to bake for their families. I knew what ingredients cost, but I hadn’t considered the many hours spent designing and decorating each cake. [19]My biggest difficulty was deciding what to charge: too little meant I worked almost for free, while too much might discourage people from ordering.[/19] Another baker gave me useful advice about calculating time as well as materials, and I now feel comfortable explaining my prices to customers.||Speaker 2 Man: I set up a service repairing computers from home. I was confident about solving technical problems, and I already had the tools I needed. The problem was that nobody outside my family knew I existed. [20]For the first few weeks, I found it extremely hard to persuade people to trust a new local repair service and give me my first paid jobs.[/20] Eventually a satisfied customer recommended me online, and things improved rapidly. Until then, I had worried the business would never really begin.||Speaker 3 Woman: My sister and I sell handmade jewellery online while studying at college. Making pieces is relaxing and uploading photographs is straightforward, but orders increased just before our exams. [21]We had to learn how to divide the week between studying, producing orders, replying to messages and posting parcels without missing deadlines in either part of our lives.[/21] We now stop taking new requests during busy college weeks. It means fewer sales, but the business has to work around our education, not take it over.||Speaker 4 Man: I began offering yoga sessions in my local community centre. Teaching classes came naturally because I had done it for years at a gym. However, running my own service required far more than teaching. [22]I had never built a website, accepted online bookings or sent digital payment requests, so learning the computer systems needed to run everything was the part I found most demanding.[/22] A friend helped me set things up, and I can now manage bookings myself without panicking every time someone changes a session.||Speaker 5 Woman: I make notebooks using recycled paper and unusual covers. At first I bought materials from one supplier, but twice my order arrived late and once the paper was a completely different thickness. [23]The challenge was finding companies that could provide the exact materials I needed consistently and deliver them before promised customer orders were due.[/23] After trying several places, I now order from two reliable suppliers. It costs slightly more, but I can make products without worrying that the right paper won't arrive.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I started making decorated cakes for birthdays after friends kept asking me to bake for their families. I knew what ingredients cost, but I hadn’t considered the many hours spent designing and decorating each cake. My biggest difficulty was deciding what to charge: too little meant I worked almost for free, while too much might discourage people from ordering. Another baker gave me useful advice about calculating time as well as materials, and I now feel comfortable explaining my prices to customers.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I set up a service repairing computers from home. I was confident about solving technical problems, and I already had the tools I needed. The problem was that nobody outside my family knew I existed. For the first few weeks, I found it extremely hard to persuade people to trust a new local repair service and give me my first paid jobs. Eventually a satisfied customer recommended me online, and things improved rapidly. Until then, I had worried the business would never really begin.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: My sister and I sell handmade jewellery online while studying at college. Making pieces is relaxing and uploading photographs is straightforward, but orders increased just before our exams. We had to learn how to divide the week between studying, producing orders, replying to messages and posting parcels without missing deadlines in either part of our lives. We now stop taking new requests during busy college weeks. It means fewer sales, but the business has to work around our education, not take it over.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: I began offering yoga sessions in my local community centre. Teaching classes came naturally because I had done it for years at a gym. However, running my own service required far more than teaching. I had never built a website, accepted online bookings or sent digital payment requests, so learning the computer systems needed to run everything was the part I found most demanding. A friend helped me set things up, and I can now manage bookings myself without panicking every time someone changes a session.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I make notebooks using recycled paper and unusual covers. At first I bought materials from one supplier, but twice my order arrived late and once the paper was a completely different thickness. The challenge was finding companies that could provide the exact materials I needed consistently and deliver them before promised customer orders were due. After trying several places, I now order from two reliable suppliers. It costs slightly more, but I can make products without worrying that the right paper won't arrive.

options.text: finding a suitable place to work
working alone rather than in a team

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker struggled to decide how much customers should pay for handmade cakes (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker's main early difficulty was getting initial customers to use the new repair service (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speakers needed to balance business duties with their studies and deadlines (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker had to learn unfamiliar digital tools for bookings, payments and a website (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker needed trustworthy suppliers who delivered suitable materials reliably (evidence [23]).
